warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.limit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
rules.sourceTitle: Food Additives DB (GSFA Online) | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions for permitted food additives in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the database and its Preamble for specific provisions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The "Codex General Standard for Food Additives" (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods...

rules.limit: Verification required via Codex standards and CCCF decisions
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) and Guideline Levels for contaminants, which are legally permitted maximum concentrations. The General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) is the primary reference.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.limit: Adherence to Code of Practice for Prevention and Reduction
rules.sourceTitle: Contaminants | CODEXALIMENTARIUS FAO-WHO
rules.notes: A specific Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) exists for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.country: European Union (Sweden, Finland, Norway, and other EU countries seeking equivalent status)
rules.foodCategory: Poultry intended for slaughter, laying hens, breeding hens, certain meat and eggs
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Special guarantees for Salmonella control are provided for Sweden, Finland, and Norway concerning trade of certain live animals and products. Other EU countries can obtain equivalent status by implementing approved control programs as per referenced regulations.
rules.evidenceQuote: When Sweden and Finland joined the EU special guarantees on Salmonella were provided as regards trade from other countries of certain live animals and products... The current guarantees are laid down in: Council Decision 95/410/EC as regards poultry intended for slaughter Decision 2004/235/EC as regards laying hens Decision 2003/644/EC as regards breeding hens Regulation (EC) No 1688/2005 as regards certain meat and eggs.

rules.sourceTitle: Database - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: The EU Additives Database serves as a tool to inform about approved food additives and their conditions of use, based on Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii), their toxins and metabolites (e.g., staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 lays down food safety criteria and process hygiene criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins, and metabolites in specific foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella , Listeria monocytogenes , Enterobacter sakazakii , staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.parameter: General labelling requirements for pre-packaged foodstuffs
rules.sourceTitle: GSO 9:2022 Labels of pre-packaged foodstuffs
rules.notes: This is a technical regulation, mandatory for pre-packaged food products.
rules.evidenceQuote: GSO 9:2022 لائحة فنية خليجية --> بطاقات المواد الغذائية المعبأة

rules.sourceTitle: GSO 2055-1:2015 Halal food - Part 1: General requirements for Halal food
rules.notes: This is a technical regulation, mandatory for products claiming to be Halal.
rules.evidenceQuote: GSO 2055-1:2015 لائحة فنية خليجية --> الأغذية الحلال – الجزء الأول : الاشتراطات العامة للأغذية الحلال

rules.parameter: Mandatory shelf life periods for food products

rules.notes: This is a technical regulation, mandatory for food products.
rules.evidenceQuote: GSO 150-1:2013 لائحة فنية خليجية --> فترات صلاحية المنتجات الغذائية - الجزء الأول : فترات الصلاحية الإلزامية

rules.notes: Verification of specific microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients is required by consulting the full GSO 1016:2015 regulation.
rules.evidenceQuote: This GSO technical regulation is concerned with microbiological limits for some foodstuffs intended for human consumption and for some food ingredients used in food industry.

rules.sourceTitle: Surat Edaran Nomor 2 Tahun 2026 tentang Ketentuan Produksi, Importasi, Registrasi, dan Peredaran Bahan Tambahan Pangan Dinitrogen Monoksida (N2O)

rules.notes: Verification required for regulations concerning foods produced by recombinant DNA techniques, potentially including labelling requirements.

rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Verification required for the fundamental principles and framework of food safety as outlined in the Food Safety Basic Law.

rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Verification required for general food sanitation requirements as stipulated by the Food Sanitation Law.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food Sanitation Law

rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Verification required for specific requirements for health claims, foods for special dietary uses, and nutrition labeling.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food with Health Claims, Food for Special Dietary Uses, and Nutrition Labeling

rules.parameter: Specifications and Standards for Microbiological Criteria in Food
rules.sourceTitle: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare: Food Safety Information
rules.notes: Verification required for specific microbiological standards for food under the Food Sanitation Act.
rules.evidenceQuote: Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: An application can be submitted to SFA to evaluate the use of an additive not currently permitted.
rules.evidenceQuote: However, you can submit an application to SFA to evaluate the use of the additive in the product that you intend to import or sell.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid or benzoic acid) are used, calculate permitted levels using the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives.
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

rules.limit: Technological justification and no health risks
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food additives are allowed when there is a technological justification and they do not present health risks to consumers.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives are allowed when: there is a technological justification they do not present health risks to consumers

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food businesses must get a list of additives used in their products, checking labels or contacting suppliers.
rules.evidenceQuote: Get a list of additives used in your products Start by checking the food labels on the packaging. Each additive is often listed by its specific name or INS/E-number. Food manufacturers must be aware of all the ingredients, including additives, that are used in the products that they manufacture. You should also reach out to the supplier to obtain more detailed information about the additives used in the products.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Check if additives are permitted using the Food Additives Search tool or the Food Additives Permitted under the Singapore Food Regulations list.
rules.evidenceQuote: Use the Food Additives Search tool . Simply type in the name of the additive to see if it is permitted under SFA's Food Regulations. Alternatively, you can download the Food Additives Permitted under the Singapore Food Regulations .

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: The food safety regulatory limits can be found in SFA's Legislation.
rules.evidenceQuote: The food safety regulatory limits can be found in SFA's Legislation .

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Different limits apply depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more.
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more about the different types of additives and the corresponding regulations that impose the limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Do not import or sell food products containing an additive that is not permitted.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food businesses must be aware of commonly found contaminants in their products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents (contaminants) in food.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents i.e. contaminants in food.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: If laboratory test results indicate contaminants, they must be within the specified limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: If laboratory test results indicate that there are contaminants in your products, they must be within the specified limits .

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Do not import or sell food products containing contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed regulatory limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Various limits are imposed on different types of contaminants (e.g., microbiological standards, heavy metals, mycotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide residues). Refer to relevant SFA documents for details.
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards Ready-to-eat food Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024) Heavy metals Heavy metals Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins Veterinary drug residues Veterinary drug residues Pesticide residues Singapore's regulatory limits Codex MRLs (if there are no regulatory limits specified in the Food Regulations) Other incidental constituents Other incidental constituents

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hexene) hexoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-n-hexane) dienoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-2, 4-hexadiene)

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: SFA has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, effective from 1 May 2020, available for download from the SFA website.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020. These MRLs can be downloaded from the SFA website at the following weblink.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: For prepacked foods, all ingredients and additives used must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Food Additives
rules.notes: Food or food products must comply with general labelling requirements.
rules.evidenceQuote: Your food or food product must also comply with the labelling requirements.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food is not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).
rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.parameter: γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) produced by microbial fermentation
rules.sourceTitle: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient
rules.notes: Specific use restrictions for γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) as a food ingredient must be verified.
rules.evidenceQuote: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient

rules.parameter: Cephalopods and cephalopod products (e.g., cuttlefish, squid, octopus); Spiral shells and spiral shell products (e.g., escargot, mussel, clam, oyster, scallop); Seeds and seed products (e.g., sunflower seed, melon seed, excluding highly refined/purified sunflower seed oil); Kiwis and kiwi products (e.g., kiwi jam, dried kiwi)
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: Declaration of these allergens is recommended if present as an ingredient or food additive in prepackaged foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: food allergen labeling is also recommended for the products that contain any of the following allergens as an ingredient or food additive

rules.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: These allergens must be declared if present as an ingredient or food additive in prepackaged foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: food allergen labeling is required for the products that contain any of the following 11 allergens as an ingredient or food additive

rules.limit: Mandatory declaration if 10 or more mg/kg
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: Sulphite must be declared if the residue sulphur dioxide is 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food.
rules.evidenceQuote: sulphite as the residue sulphur dioxide for 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food

rules.sourceTitle: Standards for Pesticide Residue Limits in Foods
rules.notes: Specific limits for pesticide residues in foods must be verified against the official standard.
rules.evidenceQuote: Standards for Pesticide Residue Limits in Foods

rules.sourceTitle: Health and Welfare Regulations Search System / National Regulations Database
rules.notes: Specific limits and requirements for various topics (additives, microbiology, contaminants, etc.) must be verified in the official regulatory databases.
rules.evidenceQuote: 法令規章 衛生福利法規檢索系統 全國法規資料庫

rules.sourceTitle: Revised Regulations on Nutrition Claim for Prepackaged Food Products
rules.notes: Specific regulations on nutrition claims for prepackaged food products must be verified.
rules.evidenceQuote: Revised Regulations on Nutrition Claim for Prepackaged Food Products

rules.limit: Suggested statement: 'The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○' or synonymous terms
rules.sourceTitle: Regulations Governing Food Allergen Labeling on the Recommended Labeling Allergens
rules.notes: If there is a risk of allergen cross-contamination, a precautionary statement is suggested.
rules.evidenceQuote: the following statement of precautionary allergen labeling is suggested: “ The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○” or the synonymous terms.

rules.parameter: γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) produced by microbial fermentation
rules.sourceTitle: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient
rules.notes: Specific labeling requirements for γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) as a food ingredient must be verified.
rules.evidenceQuote: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient

rules.limit: Verification required against the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool and relevant guidance documents/regulations.
rules.sourceTitle: Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool
rules.notes: Includes tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 CFR parts 109 and 509. Tool last updated 03/20/2025.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA has published a variety of contaminant levels for poisonous or deleterious substances in human food in various guidance documents and regulations for industry.

rules.limit: Must meet FDA's defined standard for gluten content. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods also apply.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Helps consumers with celiac disease.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling, which helps consumers, especially those living with celiac disease, be confident that items labeled “gluten-free” meet a defined standard for gluten content.

rules.limit: Not required by law and should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices. Must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Used to address unavoidable cross-contact after all precautions have been taken.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact

rules.limit: Facilities must implement allergen preventive controls as per CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117), including written procedures to control cross-contact and ensure accurate labeling.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Products found to inadvertently contain allergens due to cross-contact are considered misbranded or adulterated.
rules.evidenceQuote: The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.limit: Must be declared on the label.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.parameter: Declaration of Major Food Allergens (Contains Statement)
rules.limit: Must appear immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Example: “Contains wheat, milk, and soy.”
rules.evidenceQuote: Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.

rules.parameter: Declaration of Major Food Allergens (Ingredient List)
rules.limit: Must appear in parentheses following the name of the ingredient.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Example: “lecithin (soy),” “flour (wheat),” and “whey (milk)”.
rules.evidenceQuote: The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient.

rules.limit: Type of tree nut, species of fish, and species of Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Example: almonds, pecans, walnuts for tree nuts; bass, flounder, cod for fish; crab, lobster, shrimp for Crustacean shellfish.
rules.evidenceQuote: The law also requires that the type of tree nut (for example, almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (for example, bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (for example, crab, lobster, shrimp) to be declared.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.sourceTitle: Food Allergies
rules.notes: Must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites, due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals (e.g., asthma).
rules.evidenceQuote: Various sulfiting agents, including sodium bisulfite, are allowed as food ingredients. But due in part to adverse reactions to them, such as asthma in sensitive individuals, they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
